title: Time-Series Network Analysis
text: R package time series network analysis focus distance calculation, network construction, visualization.

title: Time-Series Network Analysis
text: can install development version tsn GitHub :

title: Time Series Network Construction — build_network
text: Build networks distance matrices using various construction methods

title: Time Series Network Construction — build_network
code: build_network(   data,   measure = "euclidean",   window = 1L,   step = 1L,   pairwise = TRUE,   symmetric = TRUE,   normalize = FALSE,   method = "full",   ... )

title: Time Series Network Construction — build_network
text: data [tsn, ts, data.frame, numeric()] Time-series data. measure [character(1)] distance measure use. window [integer(1)] Width sliding window (default 1). step [integer(1)] Step size windows (default 1) pairwise [logical(1)] TRUE (default), calculates pairwise distances windows. FALSE, uses consecutive windows . symmetric [logical(1)] TRUE (default), ensures distance matrix symmetric. FALSE, keeps edge directions pairwise = FALSE. normalize [logical(1)] TRUE, distance matrix max-normalized network construction. FALSE, uses distances (default). method [character(1)] Network construction method available options : "full": Uses raw similarity matrix (default). "knn": K-nearest neighbors network (KNN). "percentile": Percentile threshold network. "threshold": Distance threshold network. "gaussian": Gaussian kernel network. ... Additional arguments passed network construction method. include: k Number nearest neighbors (KNN method) percentile Percentile threshold (percentile method) threshold Distance threshold (threshold method) sigma Gaussian kernel parameter (Gaussian method)

title: Time Series Network Construction — build_network
text: TODO

title: Time Series Network Construction — build_network
code: set.seed(123) x <- cumsum(rnorm(100)) net <- build_network(x, window = 10, step = 10)

what: Calculate Dynamic Complexity Measures for Time-Series Data — complexity
title: Calculate Dynamic Complexity Measures for Time-Series Data — complexity
text: Computes dynamic complexity rolling window measures univariate multivariate time series data. Supports various measures including complexity, fluctuation, distribution, autocorrelation, basic statistics.

title: Calculate Dynamic Complexity Measures for Time-Series Data — complexity
Calculate Dynamic Complexity Measures for Time-Series Data — complexity
text: data [tsn, ts, data.frame, numeric()] Time-series data. measures [character()] vector measures calculate. available options : "complexity", "fluctuation", "distribution", "autocorrelation", "max", "min", "variance", "". default "complexity". See 'Details' information measures. window [integer(1)] positive integer specifying rolling window size. Must least 2. default 7. align [character(1)] Alignment window. available options : "center" (default), "right", "left". calculated measure assigned center, rightmost, leftmost point window, respectively.

title: Calculate Dynamic Complexity Measures for Time-Series Data — complexity
text: data.frame time index, original time-series data, calculated measures.

title: Calculate Dynamic Complexity Measures for Time-Series Data — complexity
text: following measures can calculated: complexity: Product fluctuation distribution measures. fluctuation: Root mean square successive differences. distribution: Deviation uniform distribution. autocorrelation: Lag-1 autocorrelation coefficient. max: Rolling maximum. min: Rolling minimum. variance: Rolling variance. option "" computes .

title: Calculate Dynamic Complexity Measures for Time-Series Data — complexity
code: # Basic complexity calculation set.seed(123) ts_data <- rnorm(100) result <- complexity(ts_data, measures = "complexity") #> Error in complexity(ts_data, measures = "complexity"): object 'fluctuation_degree' not found  # Multiple measures result_multi <- complexity(ts_data, measures = c("complexity", "variance")) #> Error in complexity(ts_data, measures = c("complexity", "variance")): object 'fluctuation_degree' not found

title: Regime Detection for Time-Series — detect_regimes
text: Detects regime changes complexity data using multiple sophisticated methods including cumulative peaks, changepoint detection, variance shifts, threshold analysis, gradient changes, entropy analysis. Provides comprehensive analysis stability  classification pattern recognition.

title: Regime Detection for Time-Series — detect_regimes
code: detect_regimes(   data,   method = "smart",   sensitivity = "medium",   min_change,   window = 10,   consolidate = FALSE,   similarity = 0.6,   peak = 1.96,   cumulative = 0.6 )

title: Regime Detection for Time-Series — detect_regimes
text: data [tsn, ts, data.frame, numeric()] Time-series data method [character(1)] Detection method. available options : "cumulative_peaks": Detects cumulative complexity peaks using Z-tests. "changepoint": Change point detection (multi-window mean-shift test). "threshold": Adaptive quartile-based regime classification. "variance_shift": Detects changes variance patterns. "slope": Detects changes local slope (rolling linear models). "entropy": Detects changes Shannon entropy complexity series, calculated rolling windows. "smart" (default): Combines gradient, peaks, changepoint methods. "": Applies individual methods listed uses ensemble voting. sensitivity [character(1)] Detection sensitivity level. available options : "low", "medium", "high". default "medium". affects thresholds window sizes within detection methods. min_change [integer(1)] Minimum number observations changes. missing (default), value determined automatically (typically 10% observations, minimum 10). window [integer(1)] base window size rolling calculations. adjusted sensitivity. default 10. consolidate [logical(1)] regimes statistically similar initial detection merged? default FALSE. similarity [numeric(1)] value 0 1 defines threshold considering regimes similar enough merge consolidate = TRUE. Based normalized mean difference. default 0.6. peak [numeric(1)] Base Z-score threshold individual peak detection "cumulative_peaks" method. Adjusted sensitivity. default 1.96. cumulative [numeric(1)] value 0 1 defines base proportion threshold identifying cumulative peak regions. Adjusted sensitivity. default 0.6.

title: Regime Detection for Time-Series — detect_regimes
text: data.frame containing original columns data additional columns regime information: change: Logical indicating regime change points id: integer regime identifier stability: Categorical stability: "Stable", "Transitional", "Unstable" complexity_pattern: Pattern description: "Increasing", "Decreasing", "Stable", "Fluctuating" stability_score: Numeric stability score (0-1) type: Type change detected method magnitude: Magnitude change (method-specific interpretation) confidence: Confidence detection (method-specific interpretation, typically 0-1)

title: Regime Detection for Time-Series — detect_regimes
code: comp <- c(   rnorm(50, 0.3, 0.05), rnorm(50, 0.8, 0.08),   rnorm(50, 0.5, 0.05), rnorm(50, 0.9, 0.1) )  regimes_all <- detect_regimes(   data = comp,   method = "all",   sensitivity = "medium" ) #> Warning: All methods failed in "all" mode.

what: Convert Time-Series Data into Wide Format Sequence Data — discretize
title: Convert Time-Series Data into Wide Format Sequence Data — discretize
text: Converts time-series data sequence data via discretization. Various methods discretization available including gaussian mixtures, K-means clustering kernel density based binning.

title: Convert Time-Series Data into Wide Format Sequence Data — discretize
code: discretize(   data,   n_states,   labels = 1:n_states,   method = "kmeans",   unused_fn = dplyr::first,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'ts' discretize(   data,   n_states,   labels = 1:n_states,   method = "kmeans",   unused_fn = dplyr::first,   ... )  # Default S3 method discretize(   data,   id_col,   value_col,   order_col,   n_states,   labels = 1:n_states,   method = "kmeans",   unused_fn = dplyr::first,   ... )

title: Convert Time-Series Data into Wide Format Sequence Data — discretize
text: data [data.frame, ts, tsn, numeric()] Either time-series data long format (data.frame, tsn), time-series object (ts), vector values. n_states [integer(1)] number states discretize data . labels [character()] vector names states. length must n_states defaults consecutive numbering, .e. 1:n_states. method [character(1)] name discretization method use. kmeans: K-means clustering (default). width: equal width binning. quantile: quantile-based binning. kde: binning based kernel density estimation. gaussian: Gaussian mixture model. unused_fn [function] handle extra columns pivoting wide format. See tidyr::pivot_wider(). default keep columns use first value. ... Additional arguments passed discretization method (stats::kmeans() kmeans, stats::density() pracma::findpeaks() kde, mclust::Mclust() gaussian). id_col [character(1)] name column contains unique identifiers. value_col [character(1)] name column contains data values. order_col [character(1)] name column contains time values (required data already order),

title: Convert Time-Series Data into Wide Format Sequence Data — discretize
text: TODO

title: Convert Time-Series Data into Wide Format Sequence Data — discretize
code: # Long format data ts_data <- data.frame(   id = gl(10, 100),   series = c(     replicate(       10,       stats::arima.sim(list(order = c(2, 1, 0), ar = c(0.5, 0.2)), n = 99)     )   ) ) data <- discretize(ts_data, "id", "series", n_states = 3)  # Time-series data data <- discretize(EuStockMarkets, n_states = 3) #> Error in data.frame(series = 1L, value = as.numeric(data), time = stats::time(data)): arguments imply differing number of rows: 1, 7440, 1860

what: Plot Time-Series Data with State Frequencies — plot_series
title: Plot Time-Series Data with State Frequencies — plot_series
text: Plot Time-Series Data State Frequencies

title: Plot Time-Series Data with State Frequencies — plot_series
code: plot_series(   data,   selected,   overlay = "v",   points = FALSE,   ncol = NULL,   max_series = 10,   trend = FALSE,   scales = c("free", "free_x", "free_y", "fixed") )

title: Plot Time-Series Data with State Frequencies — plot_series
text: data [tsn, ts, data.frame, numeric()] Time-series data plotted. selected [character()] vector indices names individual time-series plot. provided (default), time-series plotted max_series number plots. overlay [logical(1)] option plotting overlay indicates state assigned time point. Can either "h" horizontal overlay "v" vertical overlay (default). NULL, overlay plotted. points [logical(1)] point added observation? Defaults FALSE. points colored according assigned state. ncol integer giving number columns use facets. max_series [integer(1)] maximum number  time-series plot. default 10. trend [logical(1)] trend line added plot? default FALSE trend line. scales [character(1)] "fixed", "free_x", "free_y", "free" (default).

title: Plot Time-Series Data with State Frequencies — plot_series
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Time-Series Data with State Frequencies — plot_series
code: ts_data <- data.frame(   id = gl(10, 100),   time = rep(1:100,10),   series = c(     replicate(       10,       stats::arima.sim(list(order = c(2, 1, 0), ar = c(0.5, 0.2)), n = 99)     )   ) )  ts_data_disc <- discretize(ts_data, "id", "series", "time", n_states = 5) plot_series(ts_data_disc) #> Error in loadNamespace(x): there is no package called ‘ggplot2’

what: Compute trend classification based on various metrics — trend
title: Compute trend classification based on various metrics — trend
text: Calculates rolling metrics time series classifies trends ascending, descending, flat, turbulent.

title: Compute trend classification based on various metrics — trend
code: trend(   data,   window,   method = "slope",   slope = "robust",   epsilon = 0.05,   turbulence_threshold = 5,   flat_to_turbulent_factor = 1.5,   align = "center" )

title: Compute trend classification based on various metrics — trend
text: data [tsn, ts, data.frame, numeric()] Time-series data. window [integer(1)] window size metric calculation. missing, uses following adaptive sizing: max(3, min(length(data), round(length(data)/10))). method [character(1)] method trend calculation. available options ares: "slope" (default) "growth_factor". slope [character(1)] method slope calculation method = "slope". available options : "ols" (ordinary least squares), "robust" (Theil-Sen estimator), "spearman" (Spearman rank correlation based), "kendall" (Kendall's tau based). Default: "robust". epsilon [numeric(1)] threshold value defining flat trends based metric value. method = "slope", values (-epsilon, +epsilon) considered flat. "growth_factor", values (1-epsilon) (1+epsilon) considered flat. Default: 0.05. turbulence_threshold [numeric(1)] baseline threshold value classifying segment "turbulent". Based custom combined volatility metric (CV + 0.5 * range factor rolling metric values). Default: 5. flat_to_turbulent_factor [numeric(1)] multiplier turbulence_threshold assessing already "flat" segment reclassified "turbulent". value > 1 makes "flat" trends resistant becoming "turbulent". Default: 1.5. align [character(1)] Alignment window. available options : "center" (default), "right", "left". calculated metric assigned center, rightmost, leftmost point window, respectively.

title: Compute trend classification based on various metrics — trend
text: tsn object added trend column timeseries data following classes: "ascending", "descending", "flat", "turbulent", "Missing_Data", "Initial".

title: Compute trend classification based on various metrics — trend
text: Computes rolling metrics. Trend classifications ("ascending", "descending", "flat") first determined using epsilon. , "turbulent" classification can override rolling volatility metric exceeds dynamically adjusted turbulence threshold. segments initially classified "flat", threshold turbulence_threshold * flat_to_turbulent_factor, making stable reclassification "turbulent" due minor noise.

title: Compute trend classification based on various metrics — trend
code: set.seed(123) x <- cumsum(rnorm(200)) # Longer series to see more varied trends # Using a slightly larger epsilon to catch more "flat" regions tr <- trend(   x, window = 15, method = "slope",   slope = "ols", epsilon = 0.1,   turbulence_threshold = 5, flat_to_turbulent_factor = 2 )

title: The tsn Package. — tsn-package
text: Provides functions time-series network analysis including distance calculation, network construction, visualization. Provides multiple network construction methods.

title: The tsn Package. — tsn-package
text: Mohammed Saqr, Santtu Tikka, Sonsoles López-Pernas

what: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
title: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
text: Creates visibility graphs time series data, nodes represent time points edges represent visibility points according selected method. function supports Natural Visibility Graphs (NVG) Horizontal Visibility Graphs (HVG), options limited penetrable visibility temporal decay.

title: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
code: visibility_graph(   data,   method = "nvg",   directed = FALSE,   limit,   penetrable = 0,   decay_factor = 0 )

title: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
text: data [tsn, ts, data.frame, numeric()] Time-series data. method [character(1)] visibility graph construction method. options "nvg" natural visibility graphs "hvg" horizontal visibility graphs. directed [logical(1)] graph directed graph? TRUE, edges direction showing temporal order. default FALSE. limit [integer(1)] Maximum temporal distance (time steps) visibility connections. provided (default), limit applied. penetrable [integer(1)] Number points allowed penetrate visibility line Limited Penetrable Visibility Graphs (LPVG). default 0 (standard visibility graph). decay_factor [numeric(1)] Temporal decay factor edge weights. default 0 (decay). Higher values cause faster decay increasing temporal distance.

title: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
text: matrix edge weights.

title: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
text: Natural Visibility Graph (NVG): Two points can "see" straight line connecting intersect intermediate points. preserves geometric information original time series. Horizontal Visibility Graph (HVG):  Two points can "see" intermediate points strictly lower endpoints. creates sparser graphs capture different temporal patterns.

title: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
text: Limited Penetrable Visibility (LPVG): Allows specified number points penetrate visibility line, creating connected graphs. Temporal Decay: Edge weights decrease exponentially temporal distance, emphasizing local temporal relationships.

title: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
text: Lacasa, L., Luque, B., Ballesteros, F., Luque, J., & Nuño, J. C. (2008). time series complex networks: visibility graph. PNAS, 105(13), 4972-4975. Luque, B., Lacasa, L., Ballesteros, F., & Luque, J. (2009). Horizontal visibility graphs: Exact results random time series. Physical Review E, 80(4), 046103.

title: Compute a Visibility Graph from Time-Series Data — visibility_graph
code: set.seed(123) ts_data <- rnorm(50, mean = 50, sd = 15)  # Create natural visibility graph nvg <- visibility_graph(ts_data, method = "nvg")  # Create horizontal visibility graph with temporal limit hvg <- visibility_graph(ts_data, method = "hvg", limit = 10)
